summary: This interview explores ElectricSQL, an open-source platform for building local-first software that aims to eliminate the complexity of state transfer between frontend and backend. CEO James Arthur explains how ElectricSQL provides conflict-free and rollback-free programming through Transactional Causal Consistency with CRDTs, allowing developers to write to local databases without coding conflict or sync handlers. The discussion covers schema evolution challenges, where ElectricSQL only supports additive migrations to maintain finality of local writes. Arthur also discusses their choice of Elixir for the sync service, leveraging the BEAM runtime for fault tolerance and concurrency, and introduces PGLite as a future option for unified client-server data models.
key_points: ElectricSQL targets the state transfer layer, aiming to eliminate boilerplate code for serialization, caching, state management, and reactivity by making data live and reactive
The platform provides 'Finality of Local Writes' - local writes are never rejected by the server, eliminating rollbacks and making local-first development viable for mainstream applications
Schema evolution is handled through additive-only migrations, ensuring stale writes from offline clients remain valid against evolved schemas
Elixir and the BEAM runtime were chosen for the sync service due to their natural support for fault tolerance, high concurrency, and efficient stream processing
PGLite offers the prospect of unified client-server data models, enabling advanced features like pgvector embeddings and PostGIS to work identically on both ends
money_quotes: So much of web and mobile development is about ferrying data back and forth and coding across the network that the choice tends to dominate the architecture of your app and the code you have to write.
We see local-first as the best way of building pretty much all software in the future.
The delight and the promise of local-first is to be able to reduce the complexity of the development stack to just your data and your components.

Writes made to the local embedded database in the client are not subject to being rejected by the server. This gets rid of rollbacks and tentativity, which is a key advance in simplifying the local-first programming model.
Whilst the software architecture orthodoxy emphasizes the cons of tight coupling, there genuinely are both pros and cons here.
If you're building consumer, prosumer, or SaaS software and you're not building local-first, ask yourself how your product is going to compare to a product that is.
Do you want your product to feel old and laggy like Jira or super snappy and modern, like Linear and Superhuman?
key_insight: Local-first architecture with conflict-free replicated data types can eliminate the state transfer complexity that dominates modern app development, but requires accepting trade-offs in schema evolution and embracing tighter coupling between components.
